canonical: a stitch in time saves nine
meaning: Timely correction prevents bigger problems later.
origin: Recorded since Thomas Fuller's Gnomologia, 1732. Sewing metaphor: one stitch now spares nine repairs later.

variants.surface: a stitch in time saves nine

canonical: don't count your chickens before they hatch
meaning: Don't assume success before it has happened.
origin: Aesopian fable tradition; English form attested since the 16th century.

variants.surface: don't count your chickens before they hatch

variants.note: Allusive shortening, used to chide premature claims.

canonical: the early bird catches the worm
meaning: Those who act first gain the advantage.
origin: Recorded since 1605 in William Camden's Remains.

variants.surface: the early bird catches the worm

canonical: when in Rome do as the Romans do
meaning: Adapt to local customs.
origin: Attributed to St. Ambrose's advice to Augustine (4th c.); the proverbial English form by 17th c.

variants.surface: when in Rome do as the Romans do

canonical: the pen is mightier than the sword
meaning: Writing/argument has greater power than violence.
origin: Edward Bulwer-Lytton, Richelieu (1839). Now globally proverbial.

variants.surface: the pen is mightier than the sword

meaning: Behavior reveals more than rhetoric.
origin: Attested since 1736; possibly from St. Anthony of Padua.

canonical: a picture is worth a thousand words
meaning: Visual information conveys more than verbal description.
origin: Attributed to Frederick R. Barnard, 1921 (advertising). Often misattributed to Confucius.

variants.surface: a picture is worth a thousand words

canonical: too many cooks spoil the broth
meaning: Too many people involved leads to bad results.
origin: Attested since 1575; English proverb.

variants.surface: too many cooks spoil the broth

canonical: don't put all your eggs in one basket
meaning: Diversify; don't risk everything on one bet.
origin: Spanish origin (Don Quixote, 1605: 'no aventures todo tu caudal a una galera'); English by 17th c.

variants.surface: don't put all your eggs in one basket

meaning: Late action is preferable to no action.
origin: Attested since Chaucer (Canterbury Tales).

meaning: Family bonds outweigh other ties.
origin: Attested since 12th c. in Reynard the Fox tradition; the modern reading prioritizes family.
why_it_matters: Some etymological folklore claims original meaning was opposite ('blood of the covenant is thicker than water of the womb'); historians dispute this revisionist claim.

canonical: a rolling stone gathers no moss
meaning: Constant movement prevents accumulation (interpreted positively or negatively).
origin: Erasmus, Adagia (1500); from earlier Greek source. Famously ambiguous — moss as good (achievement) or bad (stagnation).

variants.surface: a rolling stone gathers no moss

variants.note: The Rolling Stones band; the magazine.

canonical: birds of a feather flock together
meaning: Similar people associate with each other.
origin: 16th c. English proverb.

variants.surface: birds of a feather flock together

canonical: the grass is always greener on the other side
meaning: Other people's situations seem better than your own.
origin: Modern English proverb; 20th c. crystallization.

variants.surface: the grass is always greener on the other side

meaning: Don't disturb a settled situation.
origin: Chaucer (Troilus and Criseyde); proverbial since.

canonical: kill two birds with one stone
meaning: Accomplish two goals with one action.
origin: Attested since the 17th century; likely earlier.

variants.surface: kill two birds with one stone

canonical: the last straw that broke the camel's back
meaning: The final small thing that causes a system to collapse.
origin: Attested since 1755 (Charles Dickens used it); from Arabic-Persian camel-loading metaphor.

variants.surface: the last straw that broke the camel's back
straw that broke the camel's back

canonical: speak of the devil and he shall appear
meaning: When you mention someone, they often appear.
origin: 16th-c. English proverb; the religious tone has been lost in modern usage.

variants.surface: speak of the devil and he shall appear

meaning: Good luck (theatrical superstition).
origin: Theater superstition; multiple competing etymologies (1920s-30s American theatre, possibly from Yiddish 'hatslokhe un brokhe').

meaning: The moment a TV show / cultural phenomenon declines into absurdity.
origin: Coined 1985 by radio personality Jon Hein referring to a 1977 Happy Days episode where Fonzie jumped a shark on water skis. Now standard cultural-criticism vocabulary.

variants.note: Past-tense form, the most common usage.

meaning: Go out and have a wild time, often involving drinking.
origin: Late 19th-c. American slang; possibly from the Marquis of Waterford's 1837 drunken rampage in Melton Mowbray, where buildings were literally painted red.

meaning: Feeling slightly ill.
origin: Nautical: sailors who were ill were sent below deck (under the weather rail).

meaning: Work or study late into the night.
origin: Francis Quarles (1635); from the era when scholars literally burned oil lamps for nighttime reading.

meaning: Get to the point; skip preliminaries.
origin: Hollywood film editing slang from the silent-era practice of cutting straight to the action/chase scene. Recorded since 1929.

meaning: Tease, deceive playfully.
origin: Recorded since the 1880s; etymological origin uncertain (one theory: 18th-c. London thieves tripping victims).

meaning: Caught in the act, with evidence on you.
origin: Scottish legal term 'red-hand,' attested since 15th c. — caught with the victim's blood still on the murderer's hands.

meaning: One who argues a contrary position for the sake of debate.
origin: From the Catholic Church's pre-1983 'advocatus diaboli' role, who argued against canonization of saints.

meaning: Learned through informal channels / gossip.
origin: American Civil War-era; 'grapevine telegraph' was the informal soldiers' news network.

meaning: Adopt a popular position once it has clearly succeeded.
origin: American 19th-c. political campaigning: literal bandwagons led parades; politicians jumped on to associate with momentum.

meaning: Time to start over.
origin: 1941 New Yorker cartoon by Peter Arno: an engineer carrying his rolled plans walks away from a crashed plane, saying the line.

canonical: at the drop of a hat
meaning: Without hesitation; at the slightest provocation.
origin: American 19th c.; dropping a hat was a starter signal for a fight or race.

variants.surface: at the drop of a hat

canonical: costs an arm and a leg
meaning: Is very expensive.
origin: American post-WWII; possibly from veteran-pension language.

variants.surface: costs an arm and a leg

meaning: Very easy.
origin: 1930s American slang; possibly from the cakewalk dance contests where the prize was a cake.

meaning: Never (used to express skepticism).
origin: 16th-c. English; the impossible scenario as the marker of 'never.'

meaning: Excessive curiosity leads to harm.
origin: Modern proverb (early 20th c.); evolved from older 'care killed the cat' (Shakespeare, Much Ado About Nothing).

meaning: Almost succeeded, but not quite.
origin: American 20th c.; from carnival games where cigars were prizes for hitting a target.

canonical: like two peas in a pod
meaning: Strikingly similar; inseparable.
origin: Attested since 16th c.; the visual metaphor of identical peas.

variants.surface: like two peas in a pod

canonical: the devil is in the details
meaning: Small details cause problems / matter most.
origin: Attributed to architect Mies van der Rohe ("God is in the details") — the devil version is a 20th-c. inversion.

variants.surface: the devil is in the details

meaning: Give up / concede defeat.
origin: Boxing terminology: the cornerman throws a towel into the ring to signal surrender.

meaning: Something seemingly bad that turns out beneficial.
origin: James Hervey's 1746 hymn; entered general English use.

meaning: An obvious problem everyone is avoiding discussing.
origin: Mid-20th-c. American; the obvious-but-unspoken thing as a giant elephant.

meaning: Cut off relationships permanently; eliminate retreat options.
origin: Military strategy of burning bridges to prevent retreat (Caesar at the Rubicon, Cortés in Mexico).

canonical: you can't judge a book by its cover
meaning: Don't form opinions based on appearance.
origin: Mid-20th c. English; before mass-market book covers, this would have made little sense.

variants.surface: you can't judge a book by its cover
don't judge a book by its cover

meaning: It's harder to do than to talk about.
origin: Attested since 15th c.
